title: Extraction and Separation of HCl and Rh(III) with Trioctylamine
abstract:
J .  Chem. Tech. Biotechnol. 1996,67,361-375 
Extraction and Separation of HCl and Rh(II1) 
with Trioctylamine 
Seyed N. Ashrafizadeh & George P. Demopoulos* 
Department of Mining and Metallurgical Engineering, McGill University, Montreal, Quebec, 
Canada H3A 2A7 
(Received 6 March 1996; revised version received 27 April 1996; accepted 31 May 1996) 
Abstract: In this paper the use of trioctylamine (TOA) to extract HCI from 
Rh(II1)-containing solutions generated by a supported liquid membrane (SLM) 
process is investigated. TOA was found to extract HCI readily (in a single 
contact of 3 min duration) at a molar ratio [HCl]/[TOA] equal to one. For each 
mole of HCl extracted an equivalent amount of H,O was found to be extracted 
as well. As far as Rh(II1) extraction of TOA is concerned this was found to 
depend on the age of the solution and the C1- concentration. Prolonged aging 
(accelerated by heating) or [Cl-] 
3 M was found to completely suppress the 
extraction of Rh(II1) by TOA. The chloride ion concentration effect was attrib- 
uted to Le Chatelier's principle while the aging effect was attributed to the 
aquation/conversion of the extractable RhCl2- complexes to RhCl,(H,0)2-. 
The aquation reaction was studied with UV-Visible spectroscopy in an effort to 
substantiate the solvent extraction (SX) results. On the basis of the findings of 
this work a combined SLM/SX process flowsheet is proposed according to which 
the Rh(II1) and HCI co-transported through the supported liquid membrane are 
co-extracted by TOA and subsequently separated by differential stripping; 
Rh(II1) with 0.5 M HC1/3 M C1- medium and HCl with NAOH. 
Key words: rhodium, trioctylamine, supported liquid membrane, solvent extrac- 
tion 
INTRODUCTION 
Rhodium (Rh) is perhaps the only primary platinum- 
group metal that still suffers from the lack of a viable 
modern 
separation 
process 
applicable 
to 
its 
purification/concentration. A number of 
research 
studies have been published recently aiming at the 
development of a solvent-based separation process.'-4 
In a departure from the conventional solvent extraction 
approach the present authors have investigated the fea- 
sibility of using a supported liquid membrane (SLM) as 
the medium to effect the separation of rhodium from 
chloride solutions. The work indeed proved successful 
and quantitative transfer of Rh(II1) was achieved 
through an SLM of Kelex 100 from a feed solution of 
high HC1 background into a similar strip solution of 
low acidity.' However, this system was found to suffer

through SLM.

Fig. 2. UV-Visible absorption spectra and calibration curve for 3-week aged Rh solutions of 2.5 M HCl. 
The permeated water results in the dilution of the SLM 
strip phase while the permeated acid aggravates further 
the problem since upon its neutralisation with a strong 
base, it produces water as well as a salt build-up in the 
SLM receiving phase. Even if the dilution of the SLM 
product solution by the co-extracted water is neglected, 
the accumulation of C1- ions into the latter phase sup- 
presses the permeation of the Rh species and tends to 
slow down and reverse the extraction proce~s.~ 
After evaluating a number of possible approaches, it 
appeared that the most appropriate way to deal with 
this problem is to withdraw selectively the permeated 
acid from the SLM product phase. To this end the 
potential of using trioctylamine (TOA) to extract and 
separate HCl from the rhodium-bearing solutions gen- 
erated by the SLM process5 has been investigated. The 
extraction of inorganic acids by various amines has 
been described in the literat~re.~-~ 
Trioctylamine 
(TOA), a well known amine with the molecular formula 
of (CH,-(CH,),),N, 
MW = 353.68, was chosen as the 
preferred amine in this work since it offers a number of 
advantages such as negligible solubility in the aqueous 
phase, high boiling point, low density (0.809)," and low 
cost and wide availability. The present work differs 
from previous studies since it describes the removal of 
HCl in the presence of Rh(II1). In particular the paper 
investigates the role of C1- concentration and age of 
solution in suppressing the co-extraction of Rh(II1) and 
it describes the main features of a novel selective acid 
removal process scheme. 
EXPERIMENTAL 
Trioctylamine (TOA) and sodium hexachlororho- 
date(II1) from Aldrich and reagent grade chloride salts, 
i.e. NH,Cl, NaC1, KC1, MgCl,, from Anachemia were 
used. Simulated SLM strip solutions were prepared by 
mixing the correct amounts of Na,RhCl, and the neces- 
sary salts with HCl of variable concentration; 0.05- 
0.30 M. Amine organic solutions were prepared by 
mixing 5% (volume basis) trioctylamine (0.1 15 M) and 
5% tridecanol (0.21 M) with kerosene. Solvent extrac- 
tion experiments were conducted in 125 cm3 separatory 
funnels. The A/O (aqueous volume/organic volume) 
ratio was kept constant at one throughout most of the 
experiments, except if otherwise stated. The majority of 
the experiments were conducted at ambient tem- 
perature, i.e. 23 k 2°C. In a few tests involving acceler- 
ated aging of the Rh solutions elevated temperatures 
(70°C) were employed. The description of the SLM 
apparatus and the procedure of the cell operation are 
reported el~ewhere.~

Seyed N. Ashrafizadeh & George P. Demopoulos*
Department of Mining and Metallurgical Engineering, McGill University, Montreal, Quebec,
(Received 6 March 1996; revised version received 27 April 1996; accepted 31 May 1996)

Rh(II1)-containing solutions generated by a supported liquid membrane (SLM)
process is investigated. TOA was found to extract HCI readily (in a single
contact of 3 min duration) at a molar ratio [HCl]/[TOA] equal to one. For each
mole of HCl extracted an equivalent amount of H,O was found to be extracted
as well. As far as Rh(II1) extraction of TOA is concerned this was found to
depend on the age of the solution and the C1- concentration. Prolonged aging
(accelerated by heating) or [Cl-]
3 M was found to completely suppress the
extraction of Rh(II1) by TOA. The chloride ion concentration effect was attrib-
uted to Le Chatelier's principle while the aging effect was attributed to the
aquation/conversion of the extractable RhCl2- complexes to RhCl,(H,0)2-.
The aquation reaction was studied with UV-Visible spectroscopy in an effort to

co-extracted by TOA and subsequently separated by differential stripping;
Rh(II1) with 0.5 M HC1/3 M C1- medium and HCl with NAOH.
Key words: rhodium, trioctylamine, supported liquid membrane, solvent extrac-
Rhodium (Rh) is perhaps the only primary platinum-
group metal that still suffers from the lack of a viable
purification/concentration. A number of
studies have been published recently aiming at the
development of a solvent-based separation process.'-4
In a departure from the conventional solvent extraction
approach the present authors have investigated the fea-
sibility of using a supported liquid membrane (SLM) as
the medium to effect the separation of rhodium from
chloride solutions. The work indeed proved successful
and quantitative transfer of Rh(II1) was achieved
through an SLM of Kelex 100 from a feed solution of
high HC1 background into a similar strip solution of
low acidity.' However, this system was found to suffer
from the problem of significant co-extraction of acid I

The permeated water results in the dilution of the SLM
strip phase while the permeated acid aggravates further
the problem since upon its neutralisation with a strong
base, it produces water as well as a salt build-up in the
SLM receiving phase. Even if the dilution of the SLM
product solution by the co-extracted water is neglected,
the accumulation of C1- ions into the latter phase sup-
presses the permeation of the Rh species and tends to
slow down and reverse the extraction proce~s.~
After evaluating a number of possible approaches, it
appeared that the most appropriate way to deal with
this problem is to withdraw selectively the permeated
acid from the SLM product phase. To this end the
potential of using trioctylamine (TOA) to extract and
separate HCl from the rhodium-bearing solutions gen-
erated by the SLM process5 has been investigated. The
extraction of inorganic acids by various amines has
been described in the literat~re.~-~
(TOA), a well known amine with the molecular formula
MW = 353.68, was chosen as the
preferred amine in this work since it offers a number of
advantages such as negligible solubility in the aqueous
phase, high boiling point, low density (0.809)," and low
cost and wide availability. The present work differs
from previous studies since it describes the removal of

investigates the role of C1- concentration and age of
solution in suppressing the co-extraction of Rh(II1) and
it describes the main features of a novel selective acid
Trioctylamine (TOA) and sodium hexachlororho-
date(II1) from Aldrich and reagent grade chloride salts,
i.e. NH,Cl, NaC1, KC1, MgCl,, from Anachemia were
used. Simulated SLM strip solutions were prepared by
mixing the correct amounts of Na,RhCl, and the neces-
sary salts with HCl of variable concentration; 0.05-
0.30 M. Amine organic solutions were prepared by
mixing 5% (volume basis) trioctylamine (0.1 15 M) and
5% tridecanol (0.21 M) with kerosene. Solvent extrac-
tion experiments were conducted in 125 cm3 separatory
funnels. The A/O (aqueous volume/organic volume)
ratio was kept constant at one throughout most of the
experiments, except if otherwise stated. The majority of
the experiments were conducted at ambient tem-
perature, i.e. 23 k 2°C. In a few tests involving acceler-
ated aging of the Rh solutions elevated temperatures
(70°C) were employed. The description of the SLM
apparatus and the procedure of the cell operation are
Extraction and separation of HC1 and Rh(ZZZ) with trioctylamine
The acidity of the aqueous solutions was measured
by standard titration methods. The amount of water in
the organic phase was measured using the Karl Fischer
technique. The amount of Rh extracted into the organic
phase was determined by difference from the feed and
raffinate concentrations which were measured by either
atomic absorption or UV-Visible spectroscopy. A UV-
Visible spectrophotometer model Cary 1 from Varian
Australia Pty Ltd was used to measure the concentra-
The absorption spectra of the Rh(II1) solutions in the
visible region (300-900 nm) were found to exhibit a
correlation between the absorption peak and the con-
centration of the Rh(II1) species (Fig. 2). This corre-
lation was found to hold independent of the HCl
concentration despite a shift in the position of the char-
acteristic absorption peak (Fig. 3). This observation
prompted the establishment and use of calibration
curves to measure the concentration of Rh in different
solutions. On the other hand, the observed shift in the
absorption band was used as a means of measuring the
progress of aquation (aging) of the chlorocomplexes of
Acid extraction and stability of TOA
Amines are well known for their capability to extract
acids. It was nevertheless necessary in this work to
measure quantitatively the degree of acid extraction
from both salt-free and salt-present solutions since the
extraction of HC1 had to be correlated to the behaviour
of rhodium. To this end, Rh-free aqueous solutions of
varying HC1 concentrations were contacted with 5%
(0.1 15 M) trioctylamine organic solutions in the absence
and presence of salt (2 M C1-). The results of these acid
extraction experiments are presented in Table 1. Quan-
titative extraction of HCl at a molar ratio [HCl]/
[TOA] of approximately one was always obtained even
at raffinate acidities as low as 0.1 M. The presence of a
high background salt concentration (2 M C1-) was
found not to influence the degree of acid extraction. The
acid extraction performance of the amine upon repeated
recycling was subsequently evaluated. The acid-loaded
organic was first stripped in one contact with
1 M NaOH, then washed twice with distilled water,
Fig. 3. UV-Visible absorption spectra for 3-week aged Rh solutions at different acidities.
Acid Extraction by TOA as a Function of Aqueous Feed Acidity
W: H,O/HCI; H,O/HC1/3 M NaCl or NH,Cl.
0: 5% Trioctylamine (0.115 M); 5% tridecanol(O.21 M ); 90% kerosene.
S. N .  Ashrafzadeh, G. P. Demopoulos
before it was used again for acid extraction. As can be
seen from the results given in Table 2, the amine solvent
showed no signs of loading-capacity loss or degradation
after a series of five cycles. In addition, phase separation
Water is known to be co-transported with acid by
basic extractants such as TOA."-l3 Since water trans-
port results in the dilution of the Rh concentration in
the product solution, it was necessary to quantify the
transported to the organic phase, in
particular in the presence of a strong salt background.
Similarly to the acid extraction experiments 0-115 M
(5%) TOA was contacted with aqueous solutions of dif-
ferent acidities, i.e. 0.1-2 M HCl. The water uptake
results which are presented in Table 3 show that under
the tested conditions water is extracted with an average
molar ratio of water/acid E 0.85, which in effect corre-
sponds to a theoretical ratio of HCl : H,O equal to one.
Apparently, the mechanism of water uptake is either via
the formation of the hydronium ions (H,O+) or, alter-
natively, via the formation of W/O microemulsions.'2
Effect of Recycling on Acid Extraction by TOA
W: 0.3 M HCl; 0.3 M HCI and 3 M NaCl or NH,CI.
0: 5% Trioctylamine (0.115 M); 5% tridecanol (0.21 M); 90%

However, in the latter case the ratio of H,O/HCl was
expected to be higher than one.
According to the above discussion, TOA has the ability
to withdraw the extra acid and water accumulated in
contain Rh complexes and this necessitated the investi-
gation of the extraction behaviour of TOA against the
Rh complexes. These investigations showed that Rh(II1)
was extracted to various degrees depending on the age
of the solution and the concentration of C1- ions (Table
4). In agreement with previously reported ~tudies,'~.'~
Rh extraction was favoured (up to 90% in a single
contact) if freshly prepared Rh solutions were used.
However, Rh extraction dropped drastically with C1-
concentration and age of the solution. Since industrial
Rh solutions are of significant age and concentrated in
terms of C1-, it can be understood why amines or the
Effect of [Cl-] and 'Age' on Rh(II1) Extraction by TOA
%Rh extracted by TOA from Rh solutions of variable age
500 ppm Rh added to solution as
0: 5% Trioctylamine (0.115 M); 5% tridecanol(O.21 M); 90% kerosene.
Extraction and separation of HC1 and Rh(ZZZ) with trioctylamine
Fig. 4. Stripping of Rh from TOA organic vs Rh concentra-
tion in the strip phase (0: 5% TOA, 10% tridecanol, kero-
sene, 450 ppm Rh, 15-min aged; S: 0-5 M HCI and 3 M NaCI,
other similar basic extractants have not proven suitable
as extractants for Rh.16 In the case presented in this
paper though the objective is not rhodium extraction
but the opposite, i.e. selective extraction of HC1 from
the SLM product phase. To this end two alternative
approaches to effect selective HCl removal were investi-
gated: (i) suppression of Rh co-extraction and (ii) co-
extraction of Rh(II1) along with HCl followed by
Table 4 clearly demonstrates that extraction of Rh(II1)
by TOA is suppressed by either a high C1- concentra-
tion (3 M) or by extended aging of the Rh feed solution
The efect of C1- concentration
The negative effect of high chloride concentration on
Rh(II1) extraction can be interpreted on the basis of Le
Chatelier’s principle. Rh(II1) extraction can be rep-
resented (in analogy to the previously well studied
extraction system Rh(II1)-HC1-Kelex 10012,” with the
3(R3NHCl) + RhC1:- -(R,NH),Rhcl,
To verify further whether the suppression of Rh(II1) is
solely the result of the high concentration of chloride
ions and not the result of any interaction between the
chloride salt, the extraction behaviour of TOA against a
number of chloride salts was examined. The salts tested
were: KCl, NaCl, NH,Cl, and MgC1,. Invariably all
salts resulted in the same result, i.e. zero extraction at 3
M C1-, i.e. it was confirmed that is the C1- concentra-
tion and not the type of cation responsible for the sup-
pression of Rh(II1) extraction.
It has been previously p o ~ t u l a t e d ~ ~ ~ ’ ~ ~ ”
relates to the conversion of the non-aquated hexachloro
rhodium(II1) complexes to the mixed aquo-chloro com-
It is indeed this phenomenon of aquation that has been
held responsible for the failure of Kelex 100 to extract
quantitatively Rh(III).” The results shown in Table 4
seem to suggest that this is also the case with tri-
octylamine. The aquation of Rh(II1) as a function of age
and temperature was studied independently in order to
provide experimental proof to the postulated suppress-
ion of Rh(II1) extraction. The monitoring of the equa-
tion process (reaction (3)) as a function of time [Cl-1,
and temperature was done with the aid of UV-Visible
spectroscopy. The results of this parallel study are sum-
On the basis of the data of Table 4 and the discussion
provided above and in the Appendix it may be con-
cluded that it is possible to effect selective extraction of
HCl with TOA as long as a high C1- concentration is
maintained in the feed solution. The required C1- con-
centration level can be lowered from 3 M down to 2 M
or so if the solution is previously subjected to aging.
The latter can be accelerated by heating (a few hours at
70°C as opposed to a few days at ambient temperature).
From the standpoint of optimum operation of the SLM
process either option is unattractive since on one hand
high C1- concentration retards significantly the per-
meation rate of rhodium5 while on the other hand pro-
longed aging especially by heating is not practical.
The alternative approach investigated was co-extraction
of Rh(II1) along HCl followed by differential stripping.
As the results of Table 4 show the extraction of Rh(II1)
is high (90% in a single contact) when the solution pro-
duced by the SLM process is still fresh. Examination by
UV-Visible spectroscopy of a Rh(II1) product solution
(0-1 M HC1) produced after 2 h operation of the SLM
apparatus did indeed show characteristic absorption
bands (Fig. 2) at the same position as with freshly pre-
pared solutions (i.e. 509 and 404 nm, respectively) which
are indicative of the presence of the non-aquated
complexes. (For more details on the UV-
Visible study of the aquation process refer to the
Appendix.) Contact of the fresh SLM strip solution with
TOA further confirmed the ability of the latter to co-
The loaded organic with HCl and RhCl2- has been
successfully subjected to differential stripping as follows.
Rhodium was stripped first through one 3-min contact
with an aqueous solution containing 0.5 M HC1/
3 M NaCl (backward direction of reaction (2)) and the
remaining acid-loaded organic was stripped through
another 3-min contact with a dilute solution of
0-5 M NaOH. Phase separation was without problems
medium to strip completely Rh(II1) from the loaded
TOA is illustrated by the results shown in Fig. 4.
According to these data 100% stripping is possible in a
single contact of 3 min, independent of the Rh concen-
tration in the strip solution. A concentration ratio of at
least 10 seems to be achieved without difficulty. On the
other hand, tridecanol was found to have a modest
effect on stripping performance (Fig. 5). Nevertheless a
minimum amount of tridecanol is needed (at least a 1/1
ratio) otherwise phase separation is poor, i.e. a third
phase forms and a small portion of the metal remains
loaded. A 2/1 (volume ratio) tridecanol/TOA ratio was
determined as offering the best performance. I
Fig. 5. Effect of tridecanol concentration on stripping of Rh
(0: 5% TOA, x% tridecanol, kerosene, 450 ppm Rh, 15-min
aged; S: 0.5 M HCI and 3 M NaCl; CT: 3 min).
S. N .  Ashrajizadeh, G. P. Demopoulos
On the basis of the findings of the present work the
flowsheet shown in Fig. 6 is proposed. According to this
flowsheet the SLM system of Rh5 is interfaced to a
TOA SX system via which the HCl co-transported with
Rh through the supported liquid membrane is removed
from the receiving SLM phase. In this way the SLM
operates at its maximum transfer rate of Rh without the
need for neutralising the receiving phase. As explained
earlier, along with HC1, RhCl2 - is co-extracted with
TOA and this is preferentially stripped with a
0.5 M HC1/3 M NaCl medium followed by HCl strip-
ping with NaOH. By interfacing the SLM system to a
downstream SX system as described in Fig. 6 the poten-
tial for achieving higher concentration of Rh and better
control of impurity carry-over exists. The SLM systems
are known to suffer from their inability to accommo-
date scrubbing of impurities which is a common prac-
tice in conventional SX circuits. In the combined
SLM/SX circuit of Fig. 6 scrubbing may be incorpor-
ated prior to and/or after stripping of rhodium from the
loaded TOA organic phase with 0.5 M HC1/3 M NaCl.
It has been demonstrated that TOA is capable of
readily and quantitatively extracting HCl from Rh(II1)-
containing chloride solutions at a molar ratio
equal to one and remains quite stable
in acid extraction cycles. Along with HCl, H,O was
found to be co-extracted at a ratio of one, i.e. either in
the form of H30+ or in the form of a W/O micro-
Rh(II1) was found to be extracted significantly only if
the solution was fresh (G2-3 h old) and the C1- con-
centration was low (0-2-0.3 M). Chloride ion concentra-
tions of about 3 M or higher were found to completely
suppress Rh(II1) extraction. Similarly, prolonged aging
suppressed Rh(II1) extraction. The 'aging' phenomenon
was studied with UV-Visible spectroscopy and it was
attributed to the aquation of the RhCl2- complexes.
From the standpoint of optimum operation of the Rh
SLM process developed and described in a previous
Fig. 6. Combined SLM/SX conceptual Rh separation flowsheet.
Extraction and separation of HC1 and Rh(ZZZ) with trioctylamine
publication5 it was concluded that co-extraction of
Rh(II1) and HCl from the SLM receiving phase with
TOA offered the best overall performance (i.e. fast
transfer rate for Rh(II1) across the SLM, concentration
of Rh(III), and opportunity for incorporating scrubbing
to overcome impurity carry-over). Rh(II1) was found to
be selectively stripped with a solution of 0.5 M HCl/
3 M C1- salt followed by stripping of HCl with a mild
By combining SLM with SX it has been shown to be
possible to overcome the intrinsic drawbacks of each of
the two separation processes and design integrated
separation circuits for metals like Rh(II1) which are dif-
ficult otherwise to extract.I6 Thus in the proposed com-
bined SLM/SX circuit, Rh(II1) is extracted thanks to the
powerful driving force of SLM which overcomes the
low distribution cofficient of Rh ( < 0 ~ 5 ) ~
makes possible the operation of the SLM at its highest
transfer (production) rate due to the continuous
removal of HCl (and Rh(II1)) from the receiving phase.
In addition, the interfacing of the TOA solvent extrac-
tion process to the SLM one makes possible the control
of impurity carry-over through the incorporation of

APPENDIX : A UV-VISIBLE INVESTIGATION
Rh(II1) in chloride solutions is known to undergo aqua-
tion resulting in the formation of mixed aquo-chloro
complexes: RhCl6 -n(H20)$3-n)-.
on the subject has been critically reviewed by Benguerel
et all6 Due to the possession of unfilled 'd' orbitals, all
of the ten isomers of Rh(II1) aquo-chlorocomplexes'*
exhibit absorption bands at the visible region" and
thus aqueous solutions are coloured. This property of
S. N .  AshraJizadeh, G. P. Demopoulos
the aquo-chlorocomplexes of Rh(II1) was used to
monitor the aquation process via the use of UV-Visible
spectroscopy. The UV-Visible absorption spectrum of
an aged (i.e. equilibrated) Rh(II1) solution is shown in
Fig. 2. The presence of two characteristic absorption
bands at approximately 400 and 500 nm, respectively is
evident. The position of the two bands was found to
shift with HC1 (Fig. 3) or C1- concentration (Table Al).
The observed shift in the wavelengths of the absorption
peaks (Fig. 3 and Table Al) was associated with the
changes in the colour of the solution, from orange-
peach to cherry-red with increasing HCl/Cl- concentra-
tion, which in turn reflect the variable degree of
aquation.16 The observed shift of one of the character-
istic absorption peaks of aged (equilibrated) Rh(II1)
solution with C1- concentration is illustrated in Fig. Al.
As can be seen, a shift towards higher wavelengths
occurs with increasing chloride ion concentration. No
shift occurs beyond 6 M. This implies that aquation
effectively stops beyond that concentration level.
The shift of the absorption bands was used to
monitor the progress of aquation (aging) in a freshly
prepared and low in Cl- concentration (0.1 M HCl)
Rh(II1) solution prepared using Na,RhCl,. The results
of this investigation are presented in Table A2. Com-
parison of these measurements with those of equili-
brated (aged) Rh(II1) solutions (Table Al) reveals the
5-min solution to be practically free of aquation (its
absorption bands correspond to an equilibrated solu-
tion of 24 M C1-, i.e. the dominant rhodium complex

The equilibration time can be shortened if the Rh(II1)
solution is subjected to heating. The effect of heating on
equilibration was studied from both sides of the equi-
librium position, i.e. aquation (forward direction of eqn
(3)) and anation (backward direction of eqn (3)). The
spectroscopic measurements are sum-
marised in Table A3. It can be seen that equilibrium
was reached within 4 h where heating was applied. The
obtained results of this investigation are in agreement
with previously published NMR data."
